content.text: Sampling from a language model involves more than picking the single most likely token at every step. Temperature is the scalar that controls how sharply the probability mass concentrates on the highest-ranked candidates, and choosing the right value determines whether an agent produces reliably structured output or exploratively diverse text. Understanding temperature from first principles — the math, the edge cases, and the interaction with other sampling knobs — is essential for building agents that behave correctly across every kind of sub-task they encounter.
What it is
Temperature is a positive scalar parameter, conventionally in the range

, applied to raw logits immediately before the softmax that converts them into a probability distribution. A logit is the unnormalised score the model assigns to each possible next token in its vocabulary. Before sampling can happen, those scores must be turned into probabilities that sum to one — and temperature is inserted at that conversion step.
Formally, given the logit vector
produced by the model for a vocabulary of size
, the probability assigned to token
at temperature
is:
When
the formula reduces to the standard softmax — no adjustment is made. When
the logits are divided by a number less than one, which amplifies differences between them: the highest-logit token's share of probability mass grows and lower-ranked tokens shrink toward zero. When
the logits are divided by a number greater than one, which compresses differences and pushes the distribution toward uniform.

Most hosted API providers expose temperature as a top-level parameter alongside max_tokens and system prompt. Open-source inference servers (vLLM, llama.cpp, Ollama) expose it under the same name. The numeric range varies slightly: Anthropic and OpenAI accept 0–1, some providers allow up to 2. At the mathematical limit as
, sampling collapses to greedy decoding (argmax). As
, the distribution becomes uniform — every token in the vocabulary is equally likely.
Why it matters

An agentic system is not a single-pass chatbot. It runs multiple LLM calls with very different requirements: a planning step that must produce structured JSON, a tool-selection step that must return a valid tool name, a reflection step that synthesises free-form prose, and a user-facing summarisation step with a natural conversational tone. A single temperature value tuned for one of those steps will degrade the others.

Concretely: if the planning step runs at temperature 0.9 and the model occasionally deviates from the required JSON schema, the downstream tool-call parser will raise an exception and the agent loop will halt — or worse, retry in a loop, burning tokens. Conversely, if the creative-writing step runs at temperature 0.0, the output will be stilted and repetitive, producing the same phrase over and over across calls. Temperature mismanagement is one of the most common sources of both reliability failures and quality regressions in agents.
Wrong temperature on structured steps
causes malformed tool calls, invalid JSON, and parse errors that crash the agent loop.
Too-low temperature on open-ended steps
produces repetitive, predictable text that users and evaluators notice as low quality.
Uniform global temperature
is a signal of an immature agent design — each node should own its parameter.
Temperature interacts with other parameters
— top-p, top-k, and frequency penalties all shift the effective distribution, so they must be tuned together.
Benefits

Temperature is the cheapest and most direct lever available for shaping generation quality. Because it is applied at inference time with no additional computation, it adds no latency or cost while dramatically changing the character of the output.
Reliability for structured outputs —
Low temperature (0–0.2) dramatically reduces the probability of the model deviating from a JSON schema or a fixed format.
Diversity for creative tasks —
Raising temperature above 1.0 lets the model surface synonyms, varied sentence structures, and unexpected framings it would otherwise suppress.
Per-node control —
Agents can assign a different temperature to each node in their graph, matching the parameter to the node's semantic role.
Reproducibility signal —
Combined with a seed, temperature 0 provides the closest available approximation to deterministic output for testing and regression detection.
Easy A/B experimentation —
Changing temperature requires editing a single field in an API call, making it simple to run offline evaluations at multiple values.
No extra cost —
Unlike chain-of-thought prompting or retrieval augmentation, temperature adjustment has zero compute overhead.
Trade-offs

Temperature is a blunt instrument. It reshapes the entire probability distribution globally, without any awareness of which parts of the vocabulary are relevant to the current task. The following limitations are important to keep in mind:
Temperature 0 is not deterministic —

Floating-point non-associativity across GPU thread orderings, driver versions, or batch sizes means two runs at temperature 0 on the same hardware may still produce different tokens on rare occasions. Never write tests that assert exact string equality based on temperature 0.
Some providers clamp or ignore temperature —

OpenAI o-series reasoning models do not accept a temperature parameter; Anthropic extended-thinking models may behave unexpectedly at temperatures other than 1.0. Always check the provider's documentation.
High temperature does not cure a bad prompt —
If the model lacks the knowledge or instruction clarity to answer well, raising temperature makes outputs more varied but not more accurate. Diversity of wrong answers is still wrong.
Low temperature can cause repetition loops —

Without a frequency penalty, very low temperature causes the model to repeatedly sample the same token sequence because it is always the argmax. Use frequency_penalty or presence_penalty alongside low temperature.
Interaction with grammar enforcement —

Constrained decoding systems (outlines, guidance, jsonformer) override the sampled token distribution entirely; temperature loses its meaning when grammar enforcement is active because only structurally valid tokens are sampled.
How it works

Every token-generation step in an autoregressive language model proceeds as follows: the model runs the current token context through its transformer layers and produces a vector of raw scores — one per vocabulary entry. These scores, called logits, encode how likely the model considers each token to appear next given the context. The logit vector is the direct output of the final linear projection layer before any normalisation.
Before the model can sample from the distribution, the logits must be normalised into probabilities. The standard mechanism is the softmax function. Temperature is injected here: instead of computing
directly, the model computes
. This is mathematically equivalent to scaling the logits before applying the exponential.
The key insight is that dividing by a small
amplifies the differences between logits because
grows exponentially. If
at temperature 1, the ratio
. At temperature 0.2, the same gap becomes
. The top token is now roughly 148 times as likely as the second-ranked one.
Entropy gives a precise measure of distribution flatness. The Shannon entropy of the token distribution at temperature
is:
At
— the distribution is a point mass on the argmax token, contributing zero bits of uncertainty. At

— the distribution is uniform and maximally uncertain. For GPT-4's vocabulary of roughly 100 000 tokens, maximum entropy is about 17 bits. Typical operating temperatures (0.7–1.0) sit at entropies between 6 and 10 bits.
After computing the probability distribution, the runtime draws a sample. Python's
numpy.random.choice

and C++ implementations both implement this as a single pass through a uniform random variate mapped to the cumulative distribution. The sampled index is then looked up in the vocabulary table to retrieve the corresponding token string.
Architecture

The diagram below shows where temperature sits in the end-to-end inference pipeline, and how the same logit vector produces radically different sampling distributions at three representative temperature values. The pipeline flows left-to-right; the bar charts below illustrate the resulting probability distributions for five hypothetical candidate tokens.

attrs.alt: Temperature in the LLM inference pipeline, with probability distributions at T=0.2, T=0.7, and T=1.5

content.text: The orange box in the pipeline — the division by T step — is the only change to standard inference. Everything else (transformer computation, softmax, multinomial sampling) is identical regardless of temperature. In the bar charts, token T1 represents the highest-logit candidate. At T=0.2 it captures 99.4% of the probability mass; at T=1.5 it holds only 58.7%, leaving meaningful probability for tokens T2 through T5.
When to use it
Use low temperature (0.0–0.3)
Generating structured data
— JSON tool call arguments, SQL queries, XML output, CSV rows.
Classification and routing
— when the model must choose from a fixed set of labels or tool names.
Code generation
— code has a syntactic contract; high temperature increases likelihood of syntax errors.
Extraction
— pulling named entities, dates, or key-value pairs from a document where the answer is factual.
Chain-of-thought reasoning steps
— logical derivations benefit from focused, consistent token choices.
Agentic plan generation
— the planner's JSON output feeds downstream parsers that have zero tolerance for schema deviations.
Use medium temperature (0.4–0.8)
General-purpose assistant responses
— conversational answers benefit from natural variation.
Summaries and paraphrases
— some lexical diversity improves readability without risking factual errors.
Email drafting and rewriting
— polished prose with slight variation across re-runs.
Use high temperature (0.9–1.5)
Brainstorming and ideation
— deliberately seeking options that the model would otherwise suppress.
Marketing copy and creative writing
— variety in phrasing matters more than strict factual accuracy.
Synthetic data generation
— creating diverse training examples from a single prompt template.
Multi-candidate sampling
— generating N responses and selecting the best with a reranker or LLM-as-judge.
When not to adjust temperature alone

Temperature alone cannot guarantee format compliance. A model at temperature 0 can still omit required JSON keys if the prompt is ambiguous. For hard format guarantees, combine low temperature with structured output enforcement: the API's
response_format
parameter (OpenAI),
tool_choice: required

(Anthropic), or constrained decoding in local inference. Do not raise temperature as a way to escape repetitive outputs without first investigating why the model is looping — the root cause is usually a missing frequency penalty or a poorly constructed prompt.
How to implement

Temperature is passed as a keyword argument in every major LLM SDK. The implementation pattern for agentic systems is to define temperature as part of the node configuration rather than as a global default, so different stages of the agent pipeline can carry their own values.
The following pattern demonstrates per-node temperature in a simple pipeline using the Anthropic Python SDK. Each function receives its own
temperature
argument, which is passed directly to the API call.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # uses ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

MODEL = "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022"


def plan_task(user_goal: str) -> dict:
    """Planning node: must return structured JSON. Use very low temperature."""
    message = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=512,
        temperature=0.1,  # near-deterministic for schema compliance
        system=(
            "You are a task planner. Return ONLY valid JSON with this shape: "
            '{"steps": [{"id": int, "action": str, "tool": str | null}]}'
        ),
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Plan how to accomplish: {user_goal}"}],
    )
    return json.loads(message.content[0].text)


def execute_step(step: dict, context: str) -> str:
    """Execution node: structured but can allow slight variation. Low-medium temperature."""
    message = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=256,
        temperature=0.3,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Execute step '{step['action']}' given context: {context}"
        }],
    )
    return message.content[0].text


def summarise_for_user(steps_output: list[str]) -> str:
    """User-facing synthesis: natural language, allow personality. Medium temperature."""
    combined = "\n".join(f"- {s}" for s in steps_output)
    message = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=300,
        temperature=0.7,  # more natural, varied phrasing
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Summarise these completed steps for the user:\n{combined}"
        }],
    )
    return message.content[0].text


# --- orchestration ---
plan = plan_task("Research the top three Python async frameworks")
results = [execute_step(step, "") for step in plan["steps"]]
summary = summarise_for_user(results)
print(summary)
Key implementation notes: always pass
temperature
explicitly — do not rely on the provider's default, which can change across SDK versions. If the node produces JSON, also add
response_format={"type": "json_object"}
where available. Log the temperature alongside each trace so that evaluation dashboards can surface correlations between temperature settings and output quality metrics.
Examples
Example 1 — Temperature sweep for structured extraction

This example demonstrates the practical effect of temperature on JSON extraction reliability. Running the same extraction prompt at multiple temperatures reveals how quickly format compliance degrades as temperature rises.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

PROMPT = (
    "Extract the following fields from the text and return valid JSON only: "
    "{name, company, role, email}. "
    "Text: 'Contact Sarah Chen, Head of Engineering at Veritas Labs, at s.chen@veritas.io'"
)

for temp in [0.0, 0.3, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5]:
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=150,
        temperature=temp,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT}],
    )
    raw = msg.content[0].text.strip()
    try:
        parsed = json.loads(raw)
        status = "OK"
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        parsed = None
        status = "PARSE ERROR"
    print(f"T={temp:.1f}  [{status}]  {raw[:80]}")

# Expected output (may vary):
# T=0.0  [OK]  {"name": "Sarah Chen", "company": "Veritas Labs", ...}
# T=0.3  [OK]  {"name": "Sarah Chen", ...}
# T=0.7  [OK]  {"name": "Sarah Chen", ...}
# T=1.0  [OK / PARSE ERROR — non-deterministic at this value]
# T=1.5  [PARSE ERROR]  Here is the extracted information: ...
Example 2 — Diversity sampling for brainstorming

Generating multiple candidate responses at a higher temperature and selecting the best is a standard technique for tasks where creativity matters. This example generates five product name suggestions and prints them all.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

BRIEF = (
    "Generate a single short product name (2–4 words) for a developer tool that "
    "automatically generates end-to-end tests from API specs. "
    "Return only the name, nothing else."
)

print("Product name candidates at T=1.2:")
names = set()
for i in range(5):
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=20,
        temperature=1.2,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": BRIEF}],
    )
    name = msg.content[0].text.strip()
    if name not in names:
        names.add(name)
        print(f"  {i+1}. {name}")

# Example output (will vary across runs — that is the point):
#   1. SpecForge
#   2. AutoSpec Sentinel
#   3. TestWeave
#   4. ApiGuard Pro
#   5. SpecRunner Zero
Real-world example

A production customer-support agent at a mid-sized SaaS company uses three LLM nodes in sequence: an intent classifier, a policy-lookup tool router, and a response composer. The team initially deployed all three at temperature 0.7 — the default they remembered from early prototyping. Production errors showed a 4% rate of malformed tool calls in the router node, causing silent failures where the agent returned a generic fallback rather than fetching the relevant policy document. Dropping the router node to temperature 0.05 eliminated malformed calls. The response composer was left at 0.7 to keep the tone conversational. The classifier was reduced to 0.2 because it only needed to return one of six intent labels.
The configuration was codified as a per-node settings dictionary, making it easy to review and adjust as new intent categories were added:

from dataclasses import dataclass
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
MODEL = "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"


@dataclass
class NodeConfig:
    name: str
    temperature: float
    max_tokens: int
    system: str


NODES = {
    "classify_intent": NodeConfig(
        name="classify_intent",
        temperature=0.05,
        max_tokens=20,
        system=(
            "Classify the user message into exactly one of: "
            "billing | technical | refund | account | feature_request | other. "
            "Return the label only."
        ),
    ),
    "route_tool": NodeConfig(
        name="route_tool",
        temperature=0.05,
        max_tokens=60,
        system=(
            "Return a JSON object with key 'tool' selected from: "
            "['get_billing_policy', 'get_tech_doc', 'get_refund_policy', 'get_account_help']. "
            "Return valid JSON only."
        ),
    ),
    "compose_response": NodeConfig(
        name="compose_response",
        temperature=0.7,
        max_tokens=300,
        system="You are a helpful, friendly support agent. Write a natural response.",
    ),
}


def call_node(config: NodeConfig, user_input: str) -> str:
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=config.max_tokens,
        temperature=config.temperature,
        system=config.system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_input}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text.strip()


def support_pipeline(user_message: str) -> str:
    intent = call_node(NODES["classify_intent"], user_message)
    tool_json = call_node(NODES["route_tool"], f"intent={intent} message={user_message}")
    response = call_node(NODES["compose_response"],
                         f"intent={intent}, tool_result_stub=..., original={user_message}")
    return response
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — Using one global temperature for all nodes

Frameworks like LangChain allow setting temperature at the LLM object level, which is then inherited by every chain that uses it. This convenient default becomes a reliability hazard when the same LLM object is reused across structurally different nodes.

# WRONG: single LLM object shared across nodes with different structural requirements
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", temperature=0.7)

json_chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Extract JSON: {text}") | llm  # needs T≈0
creative_chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Write copy: {topic}") | llm  # needs T≈1.0


# CORRECT: separate LLM instances per node role
llm_precise = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", temperature=0.05)
llm_creative = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", temperature=1.0)

json_chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Extract JSON: {text}") | llm_precise
creative_chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Write copy: {topic}") | llm_creative
Mistake 2 — Asserting exact string output at temperature 0

Many teams write unit tests that call the API at temperature 0 and assert that the exact string matches an expected value. This works on a single machine initially, then fails on CI because the cloud provider updated its infrastructure, changed floating-point kernel implementations, or altered batch scheduling. Temperature 0 gives high repeatability, not determinism. Test for semantics (does the JSON parse, does it contain the required keys, does the classification label match) rather than exact strings.
Mistake 3 — Raising temperature to fix factual errors

A model that returns incorrect factual answers will not return correct ones with higher temperature. Increasing temperature adds variance — the model may occasionally produce the right answer by chance, but the mean accuracy will stay the same or decline. Factual errors are solved by retrieval augmentation (RAG), fine-tuning, or improved prompting — not by changing temperature.
Best practices
Assign temperature per node —
store it in the node's configuration object and pass it explicitly on every API call.
Default to low for structured nodes —
start at 0.0–0.1 for any node whose output feeds a parser; relax upward only if you observe acceptable robustness.
Pair low temperature with format enforcement —
use response_format / JSON schema / tool_choice constraints alongside low temperature for defence-in-depth.
Add a frequency_penalty when temperature is near 0 —
prevents the degenerate case where the model loops on the same token sequence.
Log temperature with every trace —
include it in your observability payload so dashboards can group failure rates by temperature value.
Run offline evaluations before changing temperature in production —
even a 0.1 change can shift parse error rates by a meaningful margin on a large eval set.
Be aware of provider version drift —
pin the model version when comparing temperature settings across time, because a model update can shift the calibration of a given temperature value.
Do not guess — measure —
use a representative sample of production prompts and compute parse-success rate, format-compliance rate, and human-preference score at each temperature candidate before deciding.
Performance & scalability
Temperature is applied after the transformer forward pass, during the sampling step. The computation is a scalar division and a softmax — both
operations that are negligible compared to the
cost of attention across the context window. Changing temperature has zero impact on tokens-per-second throughput or GPU memory usage.

For latency-sensitive applications where temperature is the sole randomness source, note that best-of-N sampling — generating N completions and selecting the best — scales linearly with N in both cost and latency. At high temperature this strategy consistently outperforms a single sample, but the latency trade-off must be evaluated against the use case's SLA. Many production systems generate N=3–5 at moderate temperature (0.8–1.0) and apply a fast reranker, accepting the cost for quality-critical outputs like email drafts or code suggestions.

When running multi-agent systems at scale, note that temperature interacts with request batching. If a batch consists of a mix of tool-routing calls and creative-writing calls, those calls should be separated into distinct batches with appropriate temperatures rather than using a single batched call with a shared configuration. Most hosted APIs ignore temperature when applying cached prefixes — if your system prompt or context is cached, the temperature only affects the sampled completion tokens, which is the desired behaviour.
Tooling & ecosystem
Anthropic Python SDK

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
msg = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
    max_tokens=256,
    temperature=0.2,         # range 0.0–1.0 on Anthropic
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "List five Python async frameworks as JSON."}],
)
print(msg.content[0].text)
OpenAI Python SDK

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    temperature=0.2,          # range 0.0–2.0 on OpenAI
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "List five Python async frameworks as JSON."}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)
LangChain / LangGraph

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from typing import TypedDict

class AgentState(TypedDict):
    input: str
    plan: str
    output: str

# Per-node LLM instances with explicit temperatures
planner_llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", temperature=0.1)
writer_llm  = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", temperature=0.8)

def plan_node(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    result = planner_llm.invoke(f"Create a brief outline for: {state['input']}")
    return {**state, "plan": result.content}

def write_node(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    result = writer_llm.invoke(f"Write from this outline: {state['plan']}")
    return {**state, "output": result.content}

graph = StateGraph(AgentState)
graph.add_node("plan", plan_node)
graph.add_node("write", write_node)
graph.set_entry_point("plan")
graph.add_edge("plan", "write")
graph.add_edge("write", END)
app = graph.compile()
Local inference — Ollama / llama.cpp

# Ollama REST API — temperature as a top-level option
curl http://localhost:11434/api/generate \
  -d '{
    "model": "llama3.2",
    "prompt": "Extract entities as JSON from: Alice works at Acme Corp.",
    "options": {"temperature": 0.1, "num_predict": 100},
    "stream": false
  }'

# llama.cpp server
curl http://localhost:8080/completion \
  -d '{"prompt": "Extract entities:", "temperature": 0.1, "n_predict": 100}'
Comparison with alternatives
Temperature is not the only way to influence sampling diversity. Several other parameters and strategies exist, each with different trade-offs:
Technique
Mechanism
Range
Effect
When to prefer
Temperature
Scales all logits by 1/T before softmax
Global flattening or sharpening of the full distribution
General purpose; most widely supported
Top-p (nucleus)
Keeps smallest set of tokens summing to p
Adaptive vocabulary truncation that tracks distribution shape
Complements temperature; set p=0.95 with T=0.7
Top-k
Keeps only the k highest-probability tokens
Hard vocabulary cap regardless of probability mass
Local models via llama.cpp; older pipelines
Greedy decoding
Always picks argmax token; T effectively 0
N/A
Zero variance; guaranteed repetition without penalty
Speed benchmarks; never for deployed systems
Beam search
Expands top-k beams simultaneously
k beams
Higher-probability sequences via global tree search
Translation, summarisation; expensive; rare in agents
Frequency penalty
Penalises tokens proportional to count
Suppresses repetition independent of temperature
Always pair with low temperature to prevent loops
Security & reliability

Low temperature does not make a model more secure. A model at temperature 0 will consistently reproduce the same output for a given input — including consistently following instructions injected via prompt injection attacks. Temperature controls output variance; it does not strengthen the model's resistance to adversarial inputs. Input sanitisation, output validation, and sandboxed tool execution are the appropriate mitigations for security concerns.
For reliability specifically, temperature interacts with the following failure modes that production agents must handle:
Schema violations at moderate temperature —
Even at T=0.3 a model can emit prose before a JSON block if the prompt is not sufficiently directive. Always parse and validate the output structurally rather than trusting low temperature alone.
Non-reproducibility across deployments —

When migrating from one provider or model version to another, an established temperature value may behave differently because the underlying logit distributions shift. Re-run evaluations after any model upgrade.
Runaway loops at temperature 0 —

Agents that call themselves recursively can loop indefinitely if a low-temperature node always produces the same output that re-triggers the same condition. Implement a maximum recursion depth and a token budget guard.
Eval drift —

Evaluation harnesses that sample at T=0 may show high pass rates that collapse in production at T=0.7 because the eval did not capture the variance the production setting introduces. Always run evals at the production temperature.
Mental model

Think of temperature as the gain knob on an amplifier applied to the model's confidence. Turn the gain down (low temperature) and the model's small confidence differences between tokens become enormous — it doubles down on whatever it already slightly prefers. Turn the gain up (high temperature) and those same small differences get compressed — the model hedges, giving nearly every token some chance to appear, producing more surprising and varied output.

A useful thought experiment: imagine the model has already decided it is 60% sure the next word should be "retrieve" and 40% sure it should be "fetch". At T=0.2, those probabilities diverge to 97%/3% — "retrieve" almost always wins. At T=1.5, they converge to 52%/48% — the two options are nearly coinflips. Temperature does not change what the model knows; it changes how committed the model acts on its beliefs.
Key takeaways
Temperature divides logits before softmax, amplifying probability differences at low values and compressing them at high values. The formula is
Use temperature 0.0–0.2 for any node that must produce valid JSON, SQL, code, or a fixed label; use 0.5–0.9 for conversational or summary nodes.
Always set temperature explicitly per node in an agent graph — inheriting a single global default is a reliability anti-pattern.
Temperature 0 is high-repeatability, not deterministic: exact string tests against T=0 calls will occasionally fail across provider versions or hardware.
High temperature does not correct factual errors — it introduces variance around the same mean accuracy. Fix facts with retrieval or prompting.
Pair low temperature with format enforcement (response_format, JSON schema, or constrained decoding) for defence-in-depth; neither is sufficient alone.
Temperature adds no compute cost, but best-of-N sampling at high temperature scales linearly in cost and latency — evaluate the trade-off for your use case.
Run evals at the production temperature; eval harnesses that use T=0 miss the variance failures that will manifest in deployed systems.
